content.claim: Roma pupil segregation reaches 62% across schools where all or most pupils are Roma (ages 6–15), with pupils in segregated schools less likely to complete upper secondary education; self-reinforcing cycles occur in municipalities with multiple institutions as outflow from catchment schools increases lower-status concentration, widening the sorting effect.
content.context: Fresh live research through the School network planning lens
limitations: Segregation mechanism is settlement-level and choice-driven, not specifically attributable to six-/eight-year gimnázium tracks alone
Literature cited does not disentangle Roma segregation driven by selective tracks from that driven by general school-choice and residential patterns
No data provided on the specific share of Roma pupils in six-/eight-year vs. four-year tracks
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
